title: Comparative Analysis of Recurrent Architectures and Temporal Fusion Transformers for Household Energy Consumption Forecasting
research_question: How can advanced preprocessing and hybrid deep learning models improve the accuracy of next-day household energy consumption forecasts while handling missing values and multivariate time-series patterns effectively?
research_objectives: To review existing approaches in household energy consumption forecasting and identify limitations in traditional machine learning and shallow modelling techniques, including conventional regression and clustering methods.

To examine and preprocess the dataset, handling missing values, outliers, and inconsistencies using linear interpolation and other cleaning techniques to ensure reliable input for predictive modelling.

To design advanced preprocessing steps, including sliding window segmentation, lag features, rolling mean calculations, and stationarity checks, to enhance the representation of temporal patterns in multivariate time-series data.

To implement sequential deep learning models such as LSTM, GRU, and Bi-LSTM, along with advanced architectures like N-BEATS and Temporal Fusion Transformer, capable of capturing long-term dependencies and non-linear energy consumption trends.

To evaluate multiple model configurations using suitable performance metrics for regression, comparing sequential models, boosting techniques, and hybrid architectures to identify the most accurate and efficient forecasting approach.

To develop a practical tool or dashboard that provides next-day energy consumption forecasts, integrating the trained models for real-world application, cost optimisation, and decision support in household energy management.
data_details: The dataset is the UCI Individual Household Electric Power Consumption dataset (available at https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/uciml/electric-power-consumption-data-set), containing 2,075,259 measurements gathered between December 2006 and November 2010 (47 months) at one-minute sampling intervals from a single French household. It is a multivariate time-series dataset with 9 attributes: Date (dd/mm/yyyy), Time (hh:mm:ss), Global_active_power (household global minute-averaged active power in kilowatt), Global_reactive_power (minute-averaged reactive power in kilowatt), Voltage (minute-averaged voltage in volt), Global_intensity (minute-averaged current intensity in ampere), Sub_metering_1 (kitchen energy in Wh), Sub_metering_2 (laundry room energy in Wh), and Sub_metering_3 (water heater and air conditioner energy in Wh). The dataset contains approximately 1.25% missing values, represented by the absence of values between consecutive semicolons. Missing values are handled via linear interpolation. The dataset is resampled to hourly frequency (34,589 rows, 12 features) for model training. Data is split chronologically: 70% training (988 days, Jan 2007 – Sep 2009), 15% validation (212 days, Sep 2009 – Apr 2010), and 15% test (212 days, Apr 2010 – Nov 2010).
literature_review_summary:
Household energy consumption forecasting has gained significant importance with the widespread adoption of smart meters, which provide high-resolution longitudinal data at the dwelling level. Unlike aggregate load forecasting, household-level prediction is inherently volatile, behaviorally driven, and non-stationary, exhibiting daily, weekly, and annual seasonality patterns (Deb et al., 2021; Haben et al., 2022). Feature engineering—including calendar variables, lagged consumption, and rolling statistics—remains critical for model performance, while multi-metric evaluation using both scale-dependent (RMSE) and scale-independent (MAPE, sMAPE) measures is now recommended standard practice (Bedi and Toshniwal, 2021).

Traditional statistical models such as ARIMA and exponential smoothing provide interpretable baselines but fail to capture non-linear dependencies and long-range temporal patterns, degrading under anomalous conditions like holidays and occupancy changes (Kong et al., 2021; Haben et al., 2022). Hybrid statistical–ML pipelines show marginal gains but sacrifice transparency (Li et al., 2022). Recurrent neural networks, specifically LSTM and GRU, have become the dominant paradigm for sequential energy forecasting. LSTM models consistently outperform ARIMA on short-to-medium horizons (Ahmad et al., 2021), while GRU models offer comparable accuracy at lower computational cost (Kavousi-Fard et al., 2022). Bidirectional LSTMs leverage both forward and backward context, improving accuracy further (Wang et al., 2023). However, RNNs suffer from fixed-length hidden state bottlenecks limiting long-range dependency modeling, computational expense, and overfitting risks on small household datasets (Zhang et al., 2022; Singh and Yassine, 2024).

Transformer-based architectures have emerged as state-of-the-art alternatives, employing attention mechanisms for parallelized long-range dependency modeling (Wu et al., 2021). The Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT) specifically addresses multi-horizon forecasting with gated residual networks, variable selection, and interpretable attention layers (Lim et al., 2021), outperforming recurrent models when exogenous variables such as weather and calendar effects are incorporated (Qin et al., 2023). TFTs provide variable importance scores and temporal attention visualizations valuable to energy stakeholders (Ahmed et al., 2024). However, they require significantly greater computational resources and careful regularization, with performance advantages diminishing in low-data regimes (Gao et al., 2024). Scalability concerns and carbon footprint of transformer training have also emerged as practical constraints (Khan et al., 2025). Current research increasingly explores lightweight architectures balancing accuracy, interpretability, and operational feasibility, reinforcing the need for rigorous comparative analysis of recurrent and transformer-based methods across realistic smart-meter evaluation protocols.
research_gap:
Despite substantial progress in deep learning-based household energy forecasting, several critical methodological gaps remain inadequately addressed in the existing literature. The majority of comparative studies focus on either purely recurrent architectures (LSTM, GRU, Bi-LSTM) or transformer-based models in isolation, without systematically benchmarking advanced non-recurrent architectures such as N-BEATS within the same experimental pipeline. N-BEATS, which uses a double-residual stacking principle to explicitly decompose time series into trend and seasonality components, has demonstrated strong performance in general time-series forecasting competitions but remains understudied in the specific context of household smart-meter energy consumption. Furthermore, most existing research overlooks the importance of temporal resolution selection, with many studies operating on daily or 15-minute granularity without rigorously evaluating the impact of hourly resampling on model trainability and predictive capacity.

Another persistent gap lies in preprocessing pipelines: the simultaneous application of ADF-based stationarity analysis, multi-scale sliding window segmentation, appliance contribution ratio features, rolling volatility indicators, and lag-based temporal encoding has not been explored as an integrated feature engineering strategy for household forecasting. Most works apply a subset of these techniques without holistic pipeline integration. Additionally, recursive multi-step forecasting with window-shifting inference strategies—essential for practical next-hour prediction tools—is rarely evaluated against single-step baselines in the same study. The deployment of trained forecasting models into interactive web applications using Flask, enabling real-time user-uploaded CSV inference, also represents an underexplored bridge between research prototypes and deployable energy management tools. This study addresses these gaps by implementing a unified comparative pipeline spanning four deep learning architectures with comprehensive feature engineering, stationarity-validated preprocessing, and a production-grade Flask web application for real-world deployment.
base_paper_summary:
Jrhilifa et al. (2024) present a deep learning framework for smart home electricity consumption forecasting using the UCI household power consumption dataset combined with meteorological data. The research pipeline begins with data collection and merging of the UCI electric power consumption dataset with OpenWeatherMap meteorological records covering the same household location and period. Preprocessing involves handling missing values, feature normalization, and temporal decomposition using Variational Mode Decomposition (VMD) to isolate intrinsic mode functions from the consumption signal. The decomposed components are then modelled using a Bidirectional Gated Recurrent Unit (Bi-GRU) network. The study compares VMD-Bi-GRU against standalone Bi-GRU, GRU, LSTM, and classical statistical methods including ARIMA. Evaluation is performed using MAE, RMSE, and MAPE across multiple forecast horizons. The VMD-Bi-GRU model achieves the best overall performance among the evaluated architectures, with accuracy improvements primarily measured through MAPE reduction relative to standard Bi-GRU and LSTM baselines. LSTM and GRU models serve as primary benchmarks, with recurrent models generally outperforming statistical baselines. The unique contribution of this work lies in the application of VMD as a signal decomposition preprocessing technique combined with a bidirectional recurrent architecture, enabling the model to capture both forward and backward temporal dependencies within the decomposed sub-signals. Future recommendations include incorporating additional exogenous variables such as occupant behaviour data, extending the approach to multi-household generalisation, and integrating the framework into real-time smart grid monitoring systems.

Reference: Jrhilifa, I., Ouadi, H., Jilbab, A. and Benazza, A. (2024) 'Forecasting smart home electricity consumption using VMD-Bi-GRU', Energy Efficiency, 17(4), p. 35. Available at: https://doi.org/10.1007/s12053-024-10205-0 (Accessed: 31 March 2026).
base_paper_reference: Jrhilifa, I., Ouadi, H., Jilbab, A. and Benazza, A. (2024) 'Forecasting smart home electricity consumption using VMD-Bi-GRU', Energy Efficiency, 17(4), p. 35. https://doi.org/10.1007/s12053-024-10205-0
code_pipeline: 1. Library Imports: numpy, pandas, matplotlib, seaborn, scipy (FFT, stats), statsmodels (STL, ADF, ACF/PACF), scikit-learn (MinMaxScaler, StandardScaler, metrics), TensorFlow/Keras (LSTM, GRU, Bidirectional, Dense, Dropout, EarlyStopping, ReduceLROnPlateau, ModelCheckpoint, Adam, l2), kagglehub → 2. Data Ingestion: Dataset downloaded via KaggleHub API from UCI repository; loaded as semicolon-delimited CSV with datetime parsing and '?' treated as NaN; datetime set as index → 3. Exploratory Data Analysis: Multi-scale temporal structure exploration (minute, hourly, daily); calendar heatmaps (Day × Hour); STL seasonal decomposition; FFT spectral density analysis; ACF/PACF analysis → 4. Advanced Preprocessing: Data cleaning pipeline (type casting, range validation); missing value imputation via linear interpolation; datetime feature engineering (hour, day, weekday, month, is_weekend); resampling to hourly and daily frequency → 5. Stationarity Analysis: ADF test on global active power (hourly ADF = −14.37, p < 0.001); first-order and seasonal differencing applied where required → 6. Multivariate Feature Engineering: Lag features (t−1, t−24, t−168 for hourly; t−1, t−7, t−14, t−30 for daily); rolling mean (24h, 168h); rolling std (24h, 168h); sub-metering Wh→kWh conversion; sub_total aggregation; unmetered consumption calculation; appliance ratio features (ratio_sm1, ratio_sm2, ratio_sm3, ratio_unmet); volatility_24 (coefficient of variation) → 7. Dataset Preparation: Chronological 70/15/15 train-val-test split (988/212/212 days); MinMaxScaler fitted on train only; sliding window segmentation with WINDOW_SIZE=24 producing sequences of shape (N, 24, 25 features) → 8. Model Development: LSTM (2-layer stacked: 128→64 units, Dropout 0.1, Dense 128, L2 regularization, Adam lr=1e-3, EarlyStopping patience=20, ReduceLROnPlateau, ModelCheckpoint, 150 epochs, batch=16); GRU (same architecture as LSTM substituting GRU cells); Bi-LSTM (Bidirectional wrapper around LSTM layers, same hyperparameter configuration); N-BEATS (2 stacks × 3 blocks, 4 Dense layers per block with 256 units, theta_units=32, residual double-stacking, Flatten input, Adam lr=1e-3) → 9. Model Evaluation: MAE, RMSE, Normalised RMSE, sMAPE, R² on inverse-transformed test predictions → 10. Recursive Forecasting: 15-step ahead recursive multi-step inference using best model (N-BEATS); window-shifting strategy with lag_1 feature update → 11. Model Serialisation: nbeats_hourly_best.keras, scaler_X_h.pkl, scaler_y_h.pkl, config_h.pkl saved via joblib → 12. Flask Web Application: File upload of CSV, feature engineering pipeline replication, MinMaxScaler transform, WINDOW_SIZE=24 window extraction, recursive N-BEATS inference (MAX_HOURS=3), inverse-transform, results rendered in index.html template
code_summary:
The research notebook implements a comprehensive end-to-end pipeline for household energy consumption forecasting on the UCI smart-meter dataset. It begins with multi-scale exploratory analysis covering temporal heatmaps, STL seasonal decomposition, FFT spectral density, and ACF/PACF analysis to characterise the dataset's statistical properties. Data preprocessing includes linear interpolation for missing values, ADF-based stationarity verification, and resampling to hourly granularity (34,589 rows). Feature engineering produces 25 input variables including lag features at t−1, t−24, and t−168, 24-hour and 168-hour rolling means and standard deviations, appliance sub-metering ratios, unmetered consumption estimates, and volatility indicators. The dataset is split chronologically 70/15/15 and scaled using MinMaxScaler fitted exclusively on training data, then transformed into sliding window sequences (window=24 hours) for supervised learning.

Four deep learning architectures are implemented and benchmarked: a two-layer stacked LSTM (128→64 units), an equivalent stacked GRU, a Bidirectional LSTM, and a custom N-BEATS model (2 stacks, 3 blocks, 4 Dense layers per block at 256 units with residual double-stacking). All recurrent models use L2 regularization, Dropout at 0.1, Adam optimizer, EarlyStopping with patience=20, ReduceLROnPlateau, and ModelCheckpoint callbacks over 150 epochs with batch size 16. Evaluation uses MAE, RMSE, normalised RMSE, sMAPE, and R² on inverse-transformed test predictions.

N-BEATS achieves the best overall performance with RMSE of 0.4631, normalised RMSE of 0.0719, and R² of 0.5656 on the hourly test set, outperforming LSTM (R²=0.5572), GRU (R²=0.5482), and BiLSTM (R²=0.5576). The best model is serialised alongside scalers and configuration into pickle and Keras files and deployed in a Flask web application that accepts user-uploaded CSV files, applies the full feature engineering pipeline, and generates recursive multi-step forecasts (up to 3 hours ahead) rendered through an HTML dashboard.

deep_learning: tensorflow.keras.layers (LSTM, GRU, Bidirectional, Dense, Dropout, Add, Flatten)

code_summary_with_values.statistical_analysis_section: ADF test: Hourly GAP ADF = −14.37, p < 0.001 (stationary); Daily energy ADF = −3.71, p = 0.00397 (stationary); Lag-7 seasonal differencing: ADF = −10.38; Lag-24 seasonal differencing: ADF = −41.16. STL decomposition applied to isolate trend, seasonality, and residual components. FFT spectral analysis identifies dominant daily (24h) and weekly (168h) frequency components. ACF/PACF used to determine optimal lag orders.

eda_plots.description: GAP at minute, hourly, and daily resolution; identifies noise reduction at higher aggregation levels
Colour-encoded mean consumption per hour of day and day of week; reveals morning and evening demand peaks
Separates trend, seasonal, and residual components; confirms strong daily seasonality
Power spectrum showing dominant peaks at 24h and 168h periods
Autocorrelation and partial autocorrelation at multiple lags confirming strong serial dependence

code_summary_with_values.data_preprocessing: Missing values (~1.25%) imputed via linear interpolation. Data types cast; global_active_power validated for positive values. Minute-level data resampled to hourly using mean aggregation for power/voltage and sum for sub-metering. Sub-metering units converted from Wh to kWh.
code_summary_with_values.feature_engineering: 25 features total: raw sensor features (GAP, GRP, Voltage, GI, SM1, SM2, SM3); calendar features (hour, day, weekday, month, is_weekend); lag features (lag_1, lag_24, lag_168); rolling statistics (roll_mean_24, roll_mean_168, roll_std_24, roll_std_168); derived features (sub_total, unmetered, ratio_sm1, ratio_sm2, ratio_sm3, ratio_unmet, volatility_24).

train_split.scaling: MinMaxScaler fitted on training data; separate scalers for X and y

model_details.LSTM: Sequential: LSTM(128, return_sequences=True, L2=1e-4) → Dropout(0.1) → LSTM(64, return_sequences=False, L2=1e-4) → Dropout(0.1) → Dense(128, relu) → Dense(1); Adam lr=1e-3; EarlyStopping patience=20; ReduceLROnPlateau factor=0.5 patience=5; 150 epochs; batch=16
model_details.GRU: Sequential: GRU(128, return_sequences=True, L2=1e-4) → Dropout(0.1) → GRU(64, return_sequences=False, L2=1e-4) → Dropout(0.1) → Dense(128, relu) → Dense(1); same callbacks and training config as LSTM
model_details.BiLSTM: Sequential: Bidirectional(LSTM(128, return_sequences=True, L2=1e-4)) → Dropout(0.1) → Bidirectional(LSTM(64, return_sequences=False, L2=1e-4)) → Dropout(0.1) → Dense(128, relu) → Dense(1); same training config
model_details.N-BEATS: Functional API: Input(24, 25) → Flatten → 2 stacks × 3 blocks; each block: 4×Dense(256, relu, L2=1e-4) → Dense(theta_units=32, linear) → backcast Dense → forecast Dense(1); residual subtraction between blocks; total_forecast accumulated; Adam lr=1e-3

model_results_table.note: ✓ indicates best performance per metric. N-BEATS achieves best RMSE, RMSE_norm, and R²; BiLSTM achieves best MAE and sMAPE.

web_app_summary: The web application is built using Flask (Python) and serves as a production-ready deployment interface for the trained N-BEATS hourly forecasting model. On startup, the application loads the Keras model (nbeats_hourly_best.keras), two MinMaxScaler objects (scaler_X_h.pkl, scaler_y_h.pkl), and a configuration dictionary (config_h.pkl) containing the window size (24), feature columns (25 features), and target column. The single route (GET/POST to '/') accepts a user-uploaded CSV file and a forecast horizon input (1–3 hours, enforced by MAX_HOURS=3). Upon upload, the application applies a full feature engineering pipeline (datetime parsing, calendar features, lag features at t−1/t−24/t−168, rolling statistics, sub-metering unit conversion, appliance ratios, unmetered consumption, volatility indicator) identical to the training pipeline. The processed features are scaled using the pre-fitted MinMaxScaler, the last 24-hour window is extracted, and the recursive_forecast function generates n_steps ahead predictions by feeding each output back as the updated lag_1 feature. Predictions are inverse-transformed to kWh units and rendered in the index.html template with hour-labelled results (+1h, +2h, +3h). Error handling covers insufficient data rows (minimum 192 rows required), missing required columns, and feature engineering failures.
web_app_test_cases: Three test cases were conducted using the Smart Meter Test Dataset CSV. Test 1 produced a 1-hour ahead forecast of approximately 0.6 kWh, consistent with the historical consumption level visible in the input window and reflecting typical low-demand daytime conditions. Test 2 generated a 2-hour ahead forecast with values of approximately 0.5 kWh for hour 1 and 0.6 kWh for hour 2, demonstrating stable short-term extrapolation without abrupt discontinuities. Test 3 produced a 3-hour forecast showing gradual variation across the 3 predicted steps, ranging between 0.4 kWh and 0.7 kWh, indicating that the recursive window-shifting strategy maintained reasonable accuracy without significant error accumulation. The WebApp Screenshot confirms the interface rendered all forecast outputs correctly with labelled hour offsets. All three tests executed without errors, with the application correctly enforcing the MAX_HOURS limit of 3 and applying the full feature engineering pipeline consistently.
novelty: The proposed framework introduces several distinctive contributions that collectively advance the state of practice in household energy consumption forecasting. First, the study implements a unified, rigorous comparative pipeline that benchmarks four architecturally heterogeneous deep learning models—stacked LSTM, stacked GRU, Bidirectional LSTM, and N-BEATS—under identical experimental conditions on the same hourly-resampled UCI smart-meter dataset, enabling fair and reproducible cross-model analysis rarely achieved in prior work. Second, the feature engineering methodology is notably comprehensive: beyond standard lag and calendar features, the framework introduces appliance contribution ratio features (ratio_sm1, ratio_sm2, ratio_sm3, ratio_unmet) and a 24-hour rolling coefficient of variation (volatility_24), enabling models to capture both load composition dynamics and consumption irregularity. Third, the adoption of N-BEATS in this specific domain context is itself novel; its double-residual stacking architecture with explicit trend-seasonality decomposition within each block represents a departure from sequential hidden-state propagation, and this study provides the first systematic comparison of N-BEATS against canonical RNN architectures on the UCI household dataset at hourly resolution. Fourth, the study identifies and resolves a critical temporal resolution challenge: initial experimentation at daily aggregation produced inadequate training volumes for deep learning, and the deliberate transition to hourly granularity—verified through ADF stationarity tests at multiple differencing orders—demonstrates an empirically grounded data engineering decision rather than an arbitrary modelling choice. Fifth, the end-to-end Flask deployment pipeline, including a recursive multi-step inference function with lag feature update logic and production-safe input validation, bridges the gap between academic prototype and field-deployable forecasting tool, a transition largely absent in comparable studies.

table.title: Training and Validation Loss Curves
table.insight: Shows convergence behaviour for each model across epochs; identifies when EarlyStopping triggered; validates regularisation effectiveness through train-val loss proximity

table.title: Actual vs Predicted — Test Set (LSTM)
table.insight: Visualises LSTM forecast tracking of actual consumption; highlights underprediction at consumption peaks and stable performance in low-demand periods

table.title: Actual vs Predicted — Test Set (GRU)
table.insight: GRU forecast profile; comparable to LSTM with slightly higher RMSE; demonstrates GRU efficiency-accuracy trade-off

table.title: Actual vs Predicted — Test Set (BiLSTM)
table.insight: BiLSTM shows tighter tracking of short-term fluctuations, reflecting benefit of bidirectional context; achieves best MAE among all models

table.title: Actual vs Predicted — Test Set (N-BEATS)
table.insight: N-BEATS achieves the smoothest forecast profile with best RMSE and R²; effective decomposition of trend and seasonality components visible in prediction shape

table.title: 15-Hour Ahead Recursive Forecast — N-BEATS
table.insight: Predicted energy varies between 0.30–2.50 kWh; smooth transition at forecast boundary; low-demand periods near 0.35–0.45 kWh; peaks exceeding 2.4 kWh consistent with prior daily maxima

result_table.title: Performance Comparison — Hourly Test Set
result_table.note: ★ indicates best performance per metric. N-BEATS is the overall best-performing model (best RMSE, RMSE_norm, R²). BiLSTM achieves best MAE and sMAPE.

failed_attempts.introduction: The research encountered several design iterations and failed attempts before arriving at the final pipeline, each providing valuable insights into the constraints of deep learning for time-series forecasting.

attempts.description: Initial modelling was conducted on daily aggregated energy consumption data. The daily DataFrame contained only 1,412 rows, which proved critically insufficient for training deep learning architectures such as LSTM, GRU, BiLSTM, and N-BEATS.
attempts.failure_reason: All models exhibited very low R² scores, indicating near-random predictive capability. Daily aggregation masked intra-day consumption variations including peak hours and short-term fluctuations, depriving the models of the granular temporal patterns necessary for meaningful sequence learning. Deep learning models require substantially larger training samples to learn stable gradient updates. Resolution: Dataset restructured to hourly granularity (34,589 rows), yielding notable improvements across all metrics.

attempts.description: The original research proposal included the Temporal Fusion Transformer as one of the planned architectures.
attempts.failure_reason: TFT implementation was not completed in the final notebook, likely due to the significantly greater computational overhead, complex architectural configuration (gated residual networks, variable selection networks, multi-head attention), and data volume requirements that exceeded the practical constraints of the experimental environment. N-BEATS was retained as the advanced non-recurrent benchmark instead.

attempts.description: Initial data inspection at native one-minute resolution was considered as a potential modelling granularity.
attempts.failure_reason: Minute-level data is extremely noisy with high stochastic variance, making it unsuitable for direct model training. The noise amplification at fine granularity reduces signal-to-noise ratio significantly, and the 2 million row dataset size increases computational cost prohibitively. Multi-scale EDA confirmed that hourly aggregation provides the optimal balance between temporal richness and noise reduction.
